title: SALMON: Panel may halt sport, commercial fishing to boost supply for subsistence in Western Alaska.
summary: The Federal Subsistence Board on Wednesday will decide whether to reserve fish for rural villagers by closing sport and commercial fishing for king and chum salmon throughout most of Western Alaska. If the board does close all but subsistence fishing on federal waters within the Yukon and Kuskokwim river drainages, a fight is likely between state and federal managers. It could lead to the first standoff since the federal government took control of subsistence fishing two years ago. "The key issue here is the subsistence priority and what it means," said Tom Kron, a fisheries biologist with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service. "Things look so bad we're telling people it's likely they will get a third to half of the salmon they need for the winter. "It's serious. We have to give a priority to…

title: Do your homework before purchasing a new water filter
summary: Good water filters suck easily or they're not good water filters. Backcountry water filters include the MSR Waterworks II, the MSR Miniworks Filter, the Pur Hiker and the Sweetwater Guardian Filter. (Daily News File Photo) Nothing gets a water filter or purifier relegated to the dusty back corner of a closet faster than a difficult suck and a low flow. The two tend to go hand in hand. If the filter is hard to pump, it usually can maintain no better than a trickle of water out the hose. Some might start off producing well enough, but the effort taxes the person powering the pump, and the flow slows. Sometimes this is the fault of inferior filter design. Sometimes it's not. What is pumped into a filter directly influences how fast water flows out the other end. Not just on the first use, eit…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaskans help Smithsonian sort collection ARTIFACTS: Picking through objects stirs memories, stories. By Liz Ruskin Anchorage Daily News (Published May 15, 2001) Silver Hill, Md. -- In a Smithsonian warehouse in suburban Maryland last week, five Inupiat Eskimos from Unalakleet puzzled over boxes of old ivory charms and figurines that have sat in the Smithsonian's collection since the museum's collectors bought them in the Unalakleet area in the mid-1800s. The group was stumped by a carved medallion the diameter of a quarter but thicker. Two faces, a big one and a little one, were carved into one side. "Oh, I see," Theresa Nanouk said. "A lady packing a baby, maybe?" Her mother, in Inupiaq, suggested another idea: Maybe the carver was imitating the images from early photographs. "She wa…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Business People (Published May 14, 2001) First National Bank of Anchorage named Sue C. (Doggett) Johnson as a bank officer and promoted her to employment and recruiting manager. Johnson, who joined First National in February 1999, will oversee recruiting, interviewing and staff retention. She has experience with executive and legislative branches of government, nonprofit agencies and as a guide for photo safaris in East Africa. Eldon DeKay of Eagle River Orthodontics announced his certification as a diplomate of The American Board of Orthodontics. Certification involves a two-year residency, an exam and a case review. DeKay attended Tufts University School of Dental Medicine, received advanced training in orthodontics at the U.S/ Army program at Ft. Knox, Ky., and earned a master's in …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Tight office market makes timely renewal of lease vital By Chris Stephens (Published May 13, 2001) With today's office lease market extremely tight, it is important that tenants plan well in advance what to do when their lease expires. Otherwise they may be in trouble, not able to stay in their existing space but without new space to move into. For the past 15 years our market has had surplus space, so keeping track of a company's lease expiration date was less critical. Until now, it was not normally a problem if a company needed to stay in its space for a few months after the lease expired. In most cases, landlords would not have a new tenant lined up, so the existing tenant could make an agreement to stay longer. But that is often not the case at all today. If a tenant has not made …

title: SUBSISTENCESubsistence board meeting this week to discuss regulation changes
summary: SUBSISTENCE Subsistence board meeting this week to discuss regulation changes The Federal Subsistence Board will meet Wednesday and Thursday in the Egan Center to review proposed changes to the Federal Subsistence Management Regulations for the harvest of wildlife on federal lands in Alaska for the regulatory year beginning July 1. Although most changes to fishery regulations will be considered in December, the board will review two special action requests to close the Yukon and Kuskokwim areas to port fishing for chum and chinook salmon. The board will consider proposals that change federal determinations of customary and traditional use, open seasons, harvest limits and the methods of taking wildlife on federal public lands. The public is welcome to comment during the meeting. Submit wri…

title: Four children stabbed at Mountain View elementary school
summary: Four children were stabbed in the neck this morning by a man at Mountain View elementary school who was later shot with non-lethal projectiles and subdued by police. Injuries to two of the four children were life threatening, according to a statement by Anchorage police, and the injuries to the other two were believed to be serious. They are all currently listed in serious condition. Editor's note: This story will be updated throughout the day on adn.com, and expanded coverage will be offered in tomorrow's edition of the Anchorage Daily News. Pritchard Pupil Ashley Smith, 11, said her brothers Billy Moy, 8, and Eric Moy, 9, were both cut in their throats. The others were identified as Cody Brown, 8, and Stephan Hansell, 7. This man, something was wrong with him. He was on drugs or somethi…

title: Fishing season gets under way in Valley lakes
summary: Palmer -- Grinning broadly and chattering with excitement, 14-year-old Beau Smith's delight was palpable as he reached into a plastic bag and pulled out a rainbow trout nearly 18 inches long. Meanwhile, fishing buddy Scott Payne, 11, plucked from the same bag another, slightly smaller, ruby-sided trout. Christopher Gay of Anchorage casts flies for trout along the ice-free edges of Irene Lake.(Photo by Ken Marsh / Anchorage Daily News) The boys' tales flowed swiftly and simultaneously, like two snowmelt freshets merging into a single stream. "The best fishing is late in the evening," said Beau. "We troll in (electric-powered) motor boats." Not to be outdone, Scott added, "I caught a 23-incher on a bobber and salmon eggs." The fish was a landlocked silver salmon, he said, though sadly, neith…

title: Urban-rural tensions rise on new fronts
summary: Juneau -- Tempers flared on the state Senate floor last week when Sen. Dave Donley, R-Anchorage, offered some thoughts on human sweat and human nature. "When people invest some of their own sweat and labor into a project, they have a motive for maintaining that project," Donley said, speaking on behalf of his bill to have some rural villages begin paying part of the cost of sewer and water projects. Donley predicted a 5 percent local share could save the state $2.7 million a year. He added it is also good public policy to give villagers a sense of ownership. "It's just human nature," Donley told his colleagues. Across the room, Sen. Georgianna Lincoln, who represents a huge rural district that stretches across the Interior, stood and bristled. Once again, the icy crevasse between urban and…

title: Developers building on 1980s vision
summary: Joe and Maria Fang don't like publicity, but developers can stay low key for only so long. Last Saturday, the shy San Francisco couple found themselves donning hard hats and flinging sod with gold shovels during the ceremonial groundbreaking on an estimated $22 million Midtown Anchorage office expansion. The groundbreaking was more symbolic than anything. Building permits haven't been issued, and the real digging isn't expected to begin for at least a few weeks. But if the project does move forward, it will complete a circle that began more than 14 years ago just after the height of the 1980s building boom. The office building the Fangs want to expand, at 3000 C St. just south of Benson Boulevard, was built in 1986 to house First Interstate Bank. A few months later, the Alaska economy was …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Outdoor Hotline (Published May 13, 2001) Get ready to run/bike Eklutna Lake Challenge A new race, "The Eklutna Lake Challenge" is scheduled for 10 a.m. June 2. The race includes a 3-mile run and a 13-mile bike ride on the Lakeside Trail, an old military dirt road in Eklutna Lake State Recreation Site in Chugach State Park. The trail is hilly with a few steep grades. Bike drop off will be May 29 through June 2. Call Barbara and Danny McDonough at 1-907-746-4644 for information, or contact them at info@lifetimeadventures.net. Race brochures can be picked up at any Alaska Club, The Sports Shop, REI, Paramount Cycles, Skinny Raven Sports and Wildfire Designs in Palmer or online at www.lifetimeadventures.net. Race proceeds will benefit the Eagle River Nature Center. MEETINGS Wildlife societ…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Parks chief makes career out of wilderness, politics (Published May 13, 2001) Jim Stratton Age: 44 Occupation: State Park Director Q: What is your great outdoor accomplishment? A: Since moving to Alaska in 1981, I've managed at least one multiweek trip into the Alaskan wilderness every year but two -- a 90 percent wilderness travel success rate! When I lived in Southeast they were sea kayak trips exploring the islands and fjords of the Tongass rain forest. And when I moved to Anchorage to take a job with Alaska Conservation Foundation, I discovered the Brooks Range. In addition to being an avid outdoors enthusiast, Jim Stratton produces and hosts a radio show for KNBA, the "Arctic Cactus Hour." Since then, I've hiked or floated most of the major rivers in the Arctic National Wildlife R…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Parking project near airport stirs debate CONFLICT: Parking service is building a private lot where neighbors want a buffer zone. By Lisa Demer Anchorage Daily News (Published May 13, 2001) The world's oldest parking lot operator has cleared a parcel of woods and wetlands off International Airport Road in Spenard to put in a 1,000-vehicle lot serving long-term airport customers. Neighbors worried about traffic, lighting and the loss of natural space say they are trying to kill the project. Diamond Parking Service wants to build a 1,000-vehicle lot near the corner of International Airport Road and Northwood Drive. In this aerial photo looking south, Northwood Drive runs vertically on the right and International runs horizontally toward the top. Map below shows area from the north. (Phot…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Group saves environment by buying it NATURE CONSERVANCY: Land deals, education at heart of organization's agenda. By Paula Dobbyn Anchorage Daily News (Published May 14, 2001) It's the rare environmental group that attracts more power suits than fleece. But a Nature Conservancy reception at an Anchorage oil company headquarters last month drew a decidedly upscale crowd. Leather loafers and silk ties defined the soiree. Dapper executives from the petroleum, timber, seafood and other extractive industries sipped cocktails with bankers, Native corporation leaders, general counsels and other notables. With a high-rise view of the Chugach range warmed by an early evening sun, guests picked at baked brie, crab dip and poached salmon with dill garnish. The talk centered on market-based soluti…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska Land-Trust Groups (Published May 13, 2001) THE NATURE CONSERVANCY Date founded: 1951; first Alaska office opened in 1988 Alaska staff: 14; four Alaska offices Membership: Alaska membership: 2,000; nationwide: more than 1 million. Annual Operating Budget: $1.5 million for the Alaska office Number of deals: 39 Acres protected since founding: Over 70,000 acres. Amount spent on land acquisition: $42 million Key deals: Chilkat Bald Eagle Preserve: In 1979, the 320-acre Dalton homestead in the Chilkat River Valley is donated to the Washington chapter of The Nature Conservancey. These lands are later sold to the state of Alaska for the new Chilkat Bald Eagle Preserve. In 1993, the Conservancy added 600 acres to the preserve because of a gift from Klukwan Iron Ore Corp. Lake Clark: In 1…

summary: Alaska Money & Business Sports Outdoors Life Entertainment Obituaries Perfect World Video clips Mike Doogan Opinion Letters to the Editor Voice of the Times Forums Corrections Weather Front Page Home You can search this site, check the Daily News archives, retrieve special sections or look at the last week's news pages from the Search Page Summer Camp Guide Use this listing of camps to help plan your child's summer fun and learning. Community News Check out this free community p…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Session timing irks GOP MAY 21: Republicans say Knowles intentionally chose time inconvenient for Cowdery. By Martha Bellisle Anchorage Daily News (Published May 10, 2001) Juneau -- Gov. Tony Knowles' decision to call lawmakers back for a special session at the same time a key committee chairman's wife was scheduled for open heart surgery was "intentionally inconvenient, dangerous and inhumane," Senate President Rick Halford said Wednesday. Knowles announced late Tuesday that lawmakers must return on May 21 to finish work on a cruise ship pollution bill. The bill was stuck in Sen. John Cowdery's Transportation Committee when the Legislature adjourned just before midnight. Knowles and some lawmakers have sought monitoring and testing of the booming cruise industry since two lines were c…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Company develops kit to test for paralytic shellfish poison By Craig Medred Anchorage Daily News (Published May 13, 2001) The Kenai Peninsula's Clam Gulch is one of a few places in Alaska where it's safe to eat the clams. There has never been a case of paralytic shellfish poisoning associated with razor clams dug there. The same, unfortunately, cannot be said for other clams on other beaches in the state. Nearly everywhere else in Alaska the potentially deadly PSP toxin can be encountered in any season. There really are no safe beaches or safe times to eat clams dug in Prince William Sound, Kodiak Island, Southeast Alaska or elsewhere. But that could be about to change. A Canadian firm funded in part by a $330,000 grant from the Alaska Science and Technology Foundation has devised a co…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Skiers make haste as winter sadly withers SKIING: Last days on the slopes destined to remain among the best By Craig Medred Daily News Outdoors Editor (Published May 6, 2001) Alyeska Resort -- Off piste, in those places where a hot sun and two days of ski traffic had combined to do their worst, a foot of fresh April snow sometimes had the consistency of mashed potatoes. Snowboarder Will Murphy could not have cared less. His companions -- older, wiser, wearier twin- plankers -- tried to keep him to the easy cruising of groomed trails, but he was having none of that. Atop a good glop-touring tool, the 8-year-old boarder was in his element. He even looked covetously toward the black-diamond High Traverse. Thankfully, it was closed. The sun warming the northwest flank of 3,302-foot Max's M…

title: Real estate market likely to stay hot
summary: It's hot out there - and I don't mean the weather. I mean the real estate market! There have been more multiple offers on homes than I have seen in a long time. The typical indicators -- available inventory, sold properties and building permits -- are all up when compared with the same time last year. The market shows every indication of continued heat, especially with the recent interest rate reductions. Of the available inventory, the actual number of single-family homes for sale still remains low compared to previous years, with 627 units as of April 20. Interestingly, only 348 of the available homes were resale homes. The remaining 279 homes were under construction or recently completed, and 186 homes are purely speculative to-be-built. The average single-family sales price for the fir…

title: Natural gas pipeline would give Alaska a boost, not a boom
summary: I saw a headline the other day in an Alaska newspaper that read "Boom to follow mid-'90s bust." The construction of a new natural gas pipeline from the North Slope played a prominent role in the article that followed. That kind of hyperbole won't help Alaska policymakers, who are striving mightily to get this state's fiscal ship on an even keel. If enough people really believe a boom is coming, fiscal procrastination will continue to be the order of the day. When you talk about an employment boom in Alaska, you had better have something pretty substantial in mind. The standard was set in the early 1970s, when employment grew well in excess of 5 percent for four years after the Prudhoe Bay discovery. With the mid-1970s construction of the trans-Alaska oil pipeline, the bar was raised to a h…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Do your homework before purchasing a new water filter CHOICES: A variety of alternatives can help you avoid problems. By Craig Medred Anchorage Daily News (Published May 6, 2001) Good water filters suck easily or they're not good water filters. Nothing gets a water filter or purifier relegated to the dusty back corner of a closet faster than a difficult suck and a low flow. The two tend to go hand in hand. If the filter is hard to pump, it usually can maintain no better than a trickle of water out the hose. Some might start off producing well enough, but the effort taxes the person powering the pump, and the flow slows. Sometimes this is the fault of inferior filter design. Sometimes it's not. What is pumped into a filter directly influences how fast water flows out the other end. Not …

title: CIRI shareholders land new windfall: $15,029
summary: Shareholders of Cook Inlet Region Inc. are in line for another big check. The Anchorage-based regional Native corporation's board of directors approved a $94.3 million payout on Friday. For the typical shareholder with 100 shares, it means $15,029. The payout, stemming from CIRI's lucrative investments in wireless telecommunications nationwide, will be made May 18. It follows another huge cash distribution CIRI made in December that put $50,000 in the pockets of the average shareholder. That $314 million payment to the nearly 7,000 shareholders was the largest ever made by a Native regional corporation. The windfall comes from CIRI's stake in VoiceStream Wireless Corp. Last December, CIRI traded its partnership interests with the telecom company into 7.5 million shares of VoiceStream stock…

title: Accused attacker has history of offenses, mental disorders, self-mutilation.
summary: The man accused of stabbing four elementary school students has been talking for years about killing children while they're young, before they grow up and become sinful, according to testimony by two psychologists during a 1999 court hearing. Pritchard Jason W. Pritchard, 33, has a long history of run-ins with police in Anchorage and elsewhere in Southcentral Alaska and of harassing children on school grounds. Court files detail a history of serious psychological problems, including treatment at the Alaska Psychiatric Institute, but authorities in the past reported trouble finding out much about him. Two years ago, prosecutors reported that Pritchard was originally from Oregon, though they couldn't find any relatives there or here, and Pritchard has given a long list of home addresses in A…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Utah slide that killed Alaska doctor was likely a glide SCARS: Southcentral shows signs of the random, shearing avalanche. By Peter Porco Anchorage Daily News (Published May 6, 2001) The kind of avalanche that killed Eagle River doctor Scott Dull and his friend eight days ago in Utah occurs so randomly that it's often impossible to predict, said Anchorage avalanche specialist Jill Fredston. On the other hand, the mountains where the slide occurred, along Big Cottonwood Canyon in Wasatch Cache National Forest outside of Salt Lake City, had given signs for days that the snowpack was unstable, according to Utah authorities. Some of those signs have been seen in Southcentral Alaska, in such places as Turnagain Pass and Summit Lake, Fredston said early last week. The large avalanche that ki…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Panel seeks $2 million for oil lobbying ARCTIC POWER House Finance Committee amends capital budget. By Cathy Brown The Associated Press (Published May 7, 2001) Juneau -- Arctic Power, the nonprofit group lobbying to open the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge to oil exploration, would receive another $2 million under the House Finance Committee's version of the state capital budget. That's on top of $1.85 million legislators have already given the private group to lobby for ANWR drilling this year. In March, the Legislature passed and the governor signed an emergency spending bill that included $1.5 million "for education efforts to open the coastal plain," another $250,000 "for educational media efforts for targeted congressional districts" and $100,000 to help the North Slope village of…

title: Amendment targets credit-card fees
summary: Jerry Reinwand is a gift shop owner with a pet peeve about credit cards and an ability to do something about it. Reinwand is also a top lobbyist in Alaska and, as an innocuous rewrite of the state's banking law moved toward final passage in the Legislature this week, he made his move. Working with Sen. Robin Taylor, R-Wrangell, Reinwand slipped an amendment into the bill last week to bar credit-card companies from assessing their merchant fees -- typically 2 to 4 percent of the bill -- on sales taxes and hotel bed taxes. Merchants simply should not have to pay a fee on these taxes, he said. The banking and credit-card lobby thinks otherwise. After a version of HB 106 passed the House earlier this session, Reinwand presented his proposal to the Senate Judiciary Committee last week. Senators…

title: Best Buy shut out of Dimond Center
summary: Electronics retailer Best Buy Co. is looking to open its first Alaska store in Anchorage in the empty Alaska Marketplace site at the Dimond Center mall, but it might have to wait until next spring. The former grocery store is Best Buy's top choice, said John Buchanan, the retailer's local real estate broker. But the state attorney general's office still contends that six former Safeway stores, which had temporarily operated as Alaska Marketplace, must either sit empty or operate as supermarkets. And Best Buy doesn't sell groceries. One grocer has already gone out of business trying to run the stores, and others have looked at buying them but weren't interested. The state requirement stems from Safeway's 1999 buyout of the homegrown Carr Gottstein Foods Co., which had been Alaska's largest …

title: From 2001: Slasher wounds kids, knife-wielding assailant brings morning of terror to Mountain View
summary: The attack sent some children screaming into the school and others fighting to protect their classmates.

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Natural gas pipeline would give Alaska a boost, not a boom By David Reaume (Published May 6, 2001) I saw a headline the other day in an Alaska newspaper that read "Boom to follow mid-'90s bust." The construction of a new natural gas pipeline from the North Slope played a prominent role in the article that followed. That kind of hyperbole won't help Alaska policymakers, who are striving mightily to get this state's fiscal ship on an even keel. If enough people really believe a boom is coming, fiscal procrastination will continue to be the order of the day. When you talk about an employment boom in Alaska, you had better have something pretty substantial in mind. The standard was set in the early 1970s, when employment grew well in excess of 5 percent for four years after the Prudhoe Bay…

title: Stevens, administration strike balance on sea lions
summary: Washington -- The last roadblock to a budget deal was broken Friday afternoon when Alaska Sen. Ted Stevens and the White House compromised on a rider to protect the endangered Steller sea lion while minimizing the impact on commercial fishermen off the Republican senator's state. The agreement, which in essence phases in any new restrictions, cleared the way to final enactment of a package of spending and tax measures Friday night and the final adjournment of the lame duck congressional session. Stevens, chairman of the powerful Senate Appropriations Committee, had threatened to keep Congress in session indefinitely, and maybe deny the Clinton administration a long list of its spending priorities in its last remaining month in office, unless he won an amendment that would keep Alaska fishe…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Birder's Bowl Hundreds of bird species flock each spring to Anchorage's varied habitats By Natalie Phillips Anchorage Daily News (Published May 6, 2001) A great blue heron showed up in Anchorage this past week. So did three different species of loons, a pair of horned grebes, a killdeer and a semipalmated plover. Loons It's spring and birding in the bowl is on a roll. "It's really starting to get interesting," says the local birding hot line (338-BIRD), run by the Anchorage Chapter of the National Audubon Society. Herons are rarely seen in Anchorage. This one probably popped over from Prince William Sound, where they are also pretty rare, and settled in the south end of Potter Marsh for the day. We're thick with birds. More than 225 species have been recorded in Anchorage over the year…

title: Gwich'in leaders warn that ANWR battle's not over yet
summary: For Bob Childers, an adviser to Gwich'in Indians who oppose Arctic National Wildlife Refuge oil exploration, drilling's defeat is sweet. With oil development in ANWR seemingly dead, the Gwich'in Steering Committee is moving out of its Anchorage headquarters. Bob Childers, an adviser to the committee, started packing Thursday. (Photo by Jim Lavrakas / Anchorage Daily News) The outlook for the president's move to allow oil exploration in ANWR looks bleak. Even development's biggest supporters such as Alaska Sen. Ted Stevens say that ANWR appears dead in Congress for now. The votes aren't there. The swing senators aren't swinging. Stevens is stymied. And the Gwich'in are packing their Anchorage office and consolidating in Fairbanks. "I'm psyched," Childers pronounced. "This fall I'm going to …

title: Northern tribes survey pipeline impacts
summary: Fairbanks -- About 30 tribal leaders from Alaska and Canada were meeting in Fairbanks Wednesday to discuss the possible effect of a natural gas pipeline on Natives on both sides of the border. "We don't have the resources to deal with the issues," said Keith Van Bibber, oil and gas coordinator for the Whitehorse-based Council of First Nations. The council is a consortium of 11 Canadian tribes. Representatives from Alyeska Pipeline Service Co., the Alaska North Slope Gas Producers, and the state and federal Joint Pipeline Office are attending the meeting. The meeting was the latest of several held in Canada and Alaska, said Randy Mayo, one of the meeting's organizers. Van Bibber told the group he has been grappling with several issues since he heard about a proposed Alaska Highway route for…

title: Ad agencies turn to bears, but their message is mixed
summary: Before bopping a pushy grizzly bear in the face last week on a bridge over Wyoming's Snake river, angler Knn Bates had a TV vision. This is how it was described by Angus Thuermer Jr. of the Jackson Hole News: "Bates said he felt for a moment as if he was in 'that stupid Smirnoff commercial' in which a fisherman dukes it out with a grizzly bear." I must confess I have not seen that vodka commercial. But I have seen other advertisements lately featuring people brawling with brown bears. One of these was the promotion by John West Red Salmon of Great Britain. It featured a streamside fight between an angler and a brown bear who both wanted a tasty Alaska red salmon. It was very funny. So funny, in fact, that it became news in this newspaper. Everyone thought the commercial entertaining. In it…

title: Senate stops on Denali rail
summary: A bill moving through the Legislature would help Fairbanks entrepreneur Joseph Fields build a passenger railroad from Healy to Wonder Lake inside the wilds of Denali National Park and Preserve. The bill would transfer up to 3,500 acres of state land in an area known as the wolf townships to the Denali Borough. The transfer would provide a corridor for Field's company, Kantishna Holdings Inc., to construct the first 30 miles of the rail line to the eastern edge of the park. To continue the remaining 55 miles to Wonder Lake, the company would need an easement from the National Park Service. The bill, HB 244, passed in the House with just one no vote Friday. It was sponsored by Jeanette James, R-North Pole, who said in a written statement that the transfer would spur economic development in t…

title: Supporters don't like idea, but they're working on a plan
summary: Mayor George Wuerch thinks management of the Anchorage Museum of History and Art should probably be transferred to a private corporation and has told museum supporters to come up with a plan by next spring to privatize the city-owned institution. A committee formed by four organizations that oversee and help finance the museum has considered the question and told the mayor no. Not in the immediate future. "After studying the costs and learning from other museums' experiences, the (Future of the Museum Committee) concluded that the case for privatization of the Anchorage museum was not compelling at this time," wrote committee chairman Wilda Marston in an April 19 letter to the mayor. So, what now? Asked whether the privatization discussion is over for the time being, museum director Pat Wo…

title: Wolf hunt raises e-furor
summary: Alaska's wolf wars hit cyberspace this week with the listing of an online petition opposing a Web site auction for a guided wolf hunt near Denali National Park and Preserve. The hunt, offered on eBay by outfitter Brent Keith of Healy, is a fund-raiser for rock star Ted Nugent's Kamp for Kids, a program that gets kids out of urban areas, away from drugs and crime, and into the woods to learn how to bowhunt. According to the eBay listing, the hunter and guide will use snowmachines over five days next March to hunt "the Alaskan wolf" in an area 10 miles north of Denali park. The hunter may kill up to five wolves, the listing said. Bids closed on April 8, with the lone bidder paying $1,000. Though other Alaska guides offer wolf hunts, the Kamp for Kids' fund-raiser quickly drew fire when someo…

title: Permanent Fund checks to slide with stocks
summary: Reverberations on Wall Street shook the Alaska Permanent Fund over the past year, and for the first time since the stock market slide began, Alaskans may feel it in their checks. The Permanent Fund dividend will likely slip from $1,964 in 2000 to about $1,925 this year, according to fund projections. The slight drop in dividends obscures what has been a hard fall in the Permanent Fund's stock portfolio. Since the start of the fund's fiscal year June 30, the fund's U.S. stock holdings are down about 11 percent to about $8 billion, said Jim Kelly, spokesman for the state's $26 billion oil-wealth savings account. In the same period, an index of the 500 largest U.S. stocks fell 15 percent. In the last five months, the fund has lost nearly $2 billion in the stock market, which have been offset …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Best Buy shut out of Dimond Center ORDER: Alaska Marketplace site must go to grocery outlet or sit empty for another year. By Tony Hopfinger Anchorage Daily News (Published May 1, 2001) Electronics retailer Best Buy Co. is looking to open its first Alaska store in Anchorage in the empty Alaska Marketplace site at the Dimond Center mall, but it might have to wait until next spring. The former grocery store is Best Buy's top choice, said John Buchanan, the retailer's local real estate broker. But the state attorney general's office still contends that six former Safeway stores, which had temporarily operated as Alaska Marketplace, must either sit empty or operate as supermarkets. And Best Buy doesn't sell groceries. One grocer has already gone out of business trying to run the stores, an…

title: Excessive-force complaint takes officers off patrol
summary: Two police officers have been taken off patrol and assigned to desk jobs because a supervisor determined they used excessive force in arresting a DWI suspect, the Anchorage Police Department said Wednesday. A department investigation into the officers' actions has been turned over to state prosecutors to determine what charges, if any, to bring, APD said. Police would not name the officers or give any details about them on Wednesday. Only if they were charged criminally would their names be released, spokesman Ron McGee said. He also refused to name the sergeant who initiated the investigation. The DWI suspect, an Anchorage man, apparently was not hurt during the March arrest and did not file a complaint, McGee said. He would not discuss the case, either. The officers who arrested the man …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Urban-rural tensions rise on new fronts 'FAIRNESS': Supporters of tax move see a way to redirect money to cities. By Tom Kizzia Anchorage Daily News (Published May 4, 2001) Juneau -- Tempers flared on the state Senate floor last week when Sen. Dave Donley, R-Anchorage, offered some thoughts on human sweat and human nature. "When people invest some of their own sweat and labor into a project, they have a motive for maintaining that project," Donley said, speaking on behalf of his bill to have some rural villages begin paying part of the cost of sewer and water projects. Donley predicted a 5 percent local share could save the state $2.7 million a year. He added it is also good public policy to give villagers a sense of ownership. "It's just human nature," Donley told his colleagues. Acro…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Senate stops on Denali rail TRANSFER: Critics put brakes on land deal for northern park access. By Elizabeth Manning Anchorage Daily News (Published May 1, 2001) A bill moving through the Legislature would help Fairbanks entrepreneur Joseph Fields build a passenger railroad from Healy to Wonder Lake inside the wilds of Denali National Park and Preserve. The bill would transfer up to 3,500 acres of state land in an area known as the wolf townships to the Denali Borough. The transfer would provide a corridor for Field's company, Kantishna Holdings Inc., to construct the first 30 miles of the rail line to the eastern edge of the park. To continue the remaining 55 miles to Wonder Lake, the company would need an easement from the National Park Service. The bill, HB 244, passed in the House …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Inside Alaska Business (Published May 4, 2001) Ingersoll Hotel to shut its doors KETCHIKAN -- One of the Gateway city's oldest hotels is beginning its last season of hosting visitors. The Ingersoll Hotel will close in October after 70 years of operation and probably will reopen as a retail site, its operators said. The Waterfall Corp. bought the Ingersoll 17 years ago along with the entire city block. Waterfall General Manager Willie Herff said the hotel was sold to Ketchikan businessman John Hayes about five years ago, though Waterfall continued to operate the facility. The downturn in Southeast Alaska's timber industry means there isn't enough off-season business to keep the hotel going, Herff said. BP systems get recertification BP Exploration (Alaska) Inc. announced the environment…

title: Fire takes 18 apartments
summary: A fire Friday afternoon tore through 18 apartments in South Anchorage, displacing 42 residents and causing hundreds of thousands of dollars in damage. Flames shoot out a window at the rear of an apartment building in the Independence Park complex. Firefighters pulled out of the building for a while because of the danger. (Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) No residents were injured in the blaze at 9701 Vanguard Street in the Independence Park apartment complex, though a firefighter suffered a broken nose. At least 12 people were home at 2:50 p.m. when the fire broke out. Seven of those residents were asleep at the time and awakened by neighbors frantically trying to get everyone out of the building. Wayne Ouzts, who lives in the building across the street, said he spotted the fire breakin…

title: Big donation gives hungry dog teams chow over chums
summary: Washington -- Ralston Purina Co. is donating 22.5 tons of Dog Chow to Yukon River areas of Alaska where dog mushers are facing the grim prospect of culling their teams rather than watching them starve to death this winter because of another year of poor salmon runs. Perry Ahsogeak, director of community and natural resources for the Tanana Chiefs Conference, said that while he is grateful for the Ralston Purina food donation, it barely makes a dent in the worsening situation facing mushers along the river who were unable to catch the chum salmon they traditionally feed their dogs. "The need is very bad," Ahsogeak said. "We have about 4,000 dogs that need fish. We have over 200,000 pounds of dog food coming in, and that's about 50 pounds a dog. That won't even last a month. There's still a …

title: Whipped by gusts and scoured by dust, towns keep heads down
summary: WASILLA -- Beth McKibben's lawn chair was supposed to be unbreakable. Wind videos Knik River valley gales • High-speed users • Modem users Portage Lake area storm • High-speed users • Modem users Both clips • High-speed users • Modem users You will need RealPlayer to watch these videos. A free version is available here . Then the Valley wind got hold of it in the back yard of her Palmer home. A gust scooped up the plastic chair, cartwheeled it over her 4-foot-high fence and dropped it into the neighbor's yard. By the time McKibben caught up with it, the chair was in pieces. The chair was only a minor victim of the Valley terror, a dirt-blasting wind that residents have learned to dread. It can gust up to 100 mph, the National Weather Service says. It happens any time of year, but it is esp…

title: Painter ordered to prison
summary: A man who made a fortune as a local painting contractor was sentenced to 30 months in federal prison Thursday for knowingly hiring illegal residents, encouraging them to remain in the country and for threatening them if they alerted authorities. Eduardo Antonio Delgado, 31, himself once an illegal immigrant, was also ordered to pay $224,133 in back wages to 29 workers who helped make him a wealthy man with a mansion in South Anchorage, fancy cars and two successful businesses. The Immigration and Naturalization Service started investigating Delgado's businesses, Alaska's Finest Painting and International Drywall Inc., after the agency received tips in 1998, said John P. Gerardo, the INS special agent in charge of the case. Gerardo called it the worst case of its type in the Anchorage distr…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Inside Alaska Business (Published April 28, 2001) Ubik Corp. to purchase KMBQ-FM Ubik Corp. said Friday it has entered into an agreement to buy KMBQ-FM in Wasilla. The current owner, John Klapperich will continue as an employee of the station, said Aaron Wallender, Ubik president. "KMBQ has a long tradition of quality broadcasting and commitment to its community, two ideals we intend to continue," he said. Ubik owns KNIK-FM and operates KZND-FM, both in Anchorage. The company also manages sales of KYES-TV in Anchorage. Travelers can kick back in style Travelers to Alaska will have more lodging options this summer. A 150-room, upscale hotel is scheduled to open May 14 at the entrance to Denali National Park and Preserve. The Grande Denali Lodge will also offer six luxury cabins, a 250-s…

title: Inside Alaska Business
summary: Interior seeks review of park case The Department of the Interior has asked the 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals to reconsider whether the National Park Service must complete a full environmental review before it allows a 72 percent increase in cruise ships inside Glacier Bay National Park. The court sided with the National Parks and Conservation Association in February by ruling the Park Service erred in doing only a partial environmental review for the cruise traffic increase. Bancshares announces dividend The board of Juneau-based Alaska Pacific Bancshares has declared a quarterly dividend of 5 cents a share, payable May 17 to shareholders of record on April 30. The savings bank is traded on the over-the-counter market under the ticker symbol AKPB. RELOCATIONS Color Creek - Fiber Art C…

title: Yupik youths take a new sport to the 2,000-foot Kakooliks
summary: Daily News correspondent SAVOONGA -- Twelve-year-old Megan Penayah adjusted her parka and pulled the flaps of a thick fur hat over her cheeks before grabbing her shiny downhill skis and joining the others. Chair 2, driven by ski team assistant coach Brad Billings, pulls a group of skiers up for another run in the kakoolik Mountains. The Savoonga Alpine ski team uses a pair of snowmachines to tow the skiers as far as five miles to the slopes. (Photo by Sepp Janotta) Except for a sealskin hat or two, the skiers gathering outside the Savoonga school could have been headed to any local hill from Maine to Montana. But their pre-practice chatter, a debate over which local delicacies were best, gave them away. "Mungtuk (bowhead whale) is the bomb," said Albert Kulowiyi, a seventh-grader with a ru…

title: Portage Lake boat ban wrong
summary: The deeper one wades into the Portage Lake boat closure, the more ridiculous it becomes. Most Alaskans probably don't even know the Chugach National Forest lake is closed to boats. In fact, Alaska has a number of lakes closed to motorized watercraft for noise or environmental reasons, primarily because the lakes hold drinking water. But a closure to all boats is rare, and it's easy to miss this distinction at Portage because of the cruise ship that hauls tourists around the 21/2-mile-long lake on the hour each summer day. The cruise ship raises an important question: When is a boat not a boat? Answer: When it is a Forest Service concessionaire. The cruise ship has a special exemption to ply Portage Lake, but don't you dare put your little old canoe or kayak in the water. Oh, no, no, no, no…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Turbulent policy A new visa requirement could cost the airport millions (Published April 26, 2001) A recent change in U.S. immigration policy could cost Anchorage's airport millions of dollars in revenue and the city some jobs, according to the governor's office. In a worst-case scenario, two airlines would leave the state as a result of the new rules, said officials at Ted Stevens Anchorage International Airport. To avert that possibility, Gov. Tony Knowles has asked for an exemption. The issue centers on rules that require certain nationalities -- including Chinese, Iraqis and now Russians and Colombians -- to get U.S. visas when their flights between foreign airports stop for refueling in the United States. Anchorage is affected by another rule change that ends the visa exemption fo…

title: Slush Cup a crowd pleaser
summary: Jim Henson probably never dreamed that one of his Muppet creations would enter the Slush Cup at Alyeska Resort on Sunday afternoon. Mark Owens didn't quite make it through the Slush Cup pond. (Bill Roth / Anchorage Daily News) But there was a big blue Grover skiing down the mountain in Girdwood and attempting to skim over a pond about 80 feet long surrounded by an energetic sun-soaked crowd of about 1,500 at the marquee event of the Alyeska Spring Carnival. "Some of my friends thought I should wear it," Gary Moss said of his costume. "They said, 'You'll win for sure.' " Apparently Muppets weren't created for aerodynamics or buoyancy. Moss came up short and, aided by two divers, emerged from the pond looking more like a blue dog after a bath than a blue, cuddly Muppet. "That water is freezi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Northstar pushes for new gas well The Associated Press (Published March 7, 2001) Kenai -- A natural gas well proposed near Anchor Point could provide cheaper energy for southern Kenai Peninsula homes and businesses as well as add to the supply of gas for communities further north. Northstar Energy Group Inc. proposes drilling a second well to tap the North Fork gas field near Nikolaevsk. The first well, drilled in 1965 by Standard Oil of California, found commercial quantities of gas, said Keith Summar, Northstar vice president for exploration. However, the south Peninsula market was considered too small to justify developing the field. Northstar is betting that a pipeline soon will be built to carry south Peninsula gas to larger markets to the north. "We feel it's just a matter of tim…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Cantwell trooper serves on the edge Sgt. Sonny Sabala is community cop and wilderness patrolman By S.J. Komarnitsky Anchorage Daily News (Published April 29, 2001) Cantwell -- Sonny Sabala can't pinpoint when he knew he had found a home as the state trooper on this remote stretch of the Parks Highway. But it may have roughly coincided with the appearance of the 300-pound man in a dress. Sabala found the man on a summer day sprawled across the road next to the Longhorn Bar. His bulk blocked the two-lane street as effectively as a jackknifed semi. A crowd of young revelers gathered around. With some arm-twisting and cajoling, Sabala pried the man off the pavement and stuffed him into the back seat of the patrol car. Then he braced for what six years in law enforcement told him was coming…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Governor protests panel's halibut quota decision SPORT FISHING: Council approves catch quotas for Alaska charter boats. By Wesley Loy Anchorage Daily News (Published April 27, 2001) Alaska Gov. Tony Knowles is urging federal officials to reject an advisory council's vote to assign private catch shares to some 1,100 halibut charter boats. Knowles this week sent letters to Commerce Secretary Don Evans and Sen. Ted Stevens, R-Alaska, urging them to act against the plan. "I think you have to have a compelling reason to change a public resource to private ownership," Knowles said in an interview Thursday. The North Pacific Fishery Management Council on April 14 approved the controversial individual quota program for Southeast and Southcentral halibut charter boats. The panel, made up of com…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Quake diverts Anchorage travelers AIR TRAFFIC: Cracks are found in Sea-Tac runway, but flights resume as passengers stack up. By Doug O'harra Anchorage Daily News (Published March 1, 2001) The emergency closure of the Seattle Tacoma International Airport following the Wednesday earthquake derailed or delayed thousands of people traveling to or from Alaska. But most flights linked with Anchorage appeared to be running by late afternoon. Control tower windows at Seattle Tacoma International Airport were knocked out by the quake. Many flights, including one about to land, were diverted after the dust settled. (Greg Gilbert / The Seattle Times) The quake damaged Sea-Tac's control tower, but a temporary one was set up, and the Federal Aviation Administration began allowing some flights afte…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | EPA says processors polluted FINES Companies face $132,000 in fines for handling of waste. By Wesley Loy Anchorage Daily News (Published March 9, 2001) The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency is proposing a total of $132,000 in fines against two seafood processing companies accused of improperly discharging fish waste from plants in Ketchikan. The Northwest regional office of the EPA in Seattle proposes $77,000 in fines against Wards Cove Packing Co. and $55,000 against NorQuest Seafoods Inc. Both companies are based in Seattle. EPA officials accuse Wards Cove of failing to route all seafood waste through a waste-handling system, discharging seafood waste exceeding a half-inch in size, accumulating seafood waste not routed through the treatment system on the sea floor beneath the plan…

title: ALERTSTrails closed to bikes, horses
summary: ALERTS Trails closed to bikes, horses Kincaid Park and Hillside Park trails are closed to mountain biking and horseback riding until June 1 or further notice. Park managers hope to limit erosion problems. For information call 343-6397. EDUCATION Camp offers summer fun for kids Kids can learn about hiking, animal tracking, photography, kite flying, map making, orienteering and beach combing at the city-sponsored Kincaid Outdoor Adventure Camp. Registration is now open. Weekly sessions run from 8:30 to 11:30 a.m. beginning June 11 and continue through Aug. 24. The cost is $60 a week per child. Call 343-6397 for more information. FISHING Boy Scouts say no to PETA request People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals has asked the Boy Scouts of America to retire fishing and fish and wildlife man…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Kodiak eyed for missile defense tests PROPOSAL: Pentagon considers using site as it plans about 20 more launches in testing the system. The Associated Press (Published April 6, 2001) Kodiak -- A recently retired Pentagon official made a recommendation that could bring some of the national missile defense system test launches to Kodiak's rocket facility. Phil Coyle, who retired in January as director of operational test and evaluation for the Defense Department, said Wednesday he recommended that there be a greater separation between launch and radar sites during tests of the national missile defense system. Mock enemy missiles have been launched from Vandenberg Air Force Base on the California coast north of Santa Barbara. They have been tracked by an early warning radar at Beale Air F…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Shorter cruises offered BON VOYAGE: Spring, fall sailings position ships for tourist season. The Associated Press (Published April 25, 2001) Two cruise lines operating in Alaska are planning shorter voyages in the Northwest this season. The short cruises allow the companies to make some money at the beginning and end of the Alaska season as they get their ships into position for longer cruises. Last year, Royal Caribbean International launched its three- and four-night cruises from Seattle. The company plans to offer the packages again this year, using its new 2,100-passenger Radiance of the Seas. Princess Cruises will begin offering three- and four-day round-trip voyages from Vancouver, British Columbia, that include a stop in either Ketchikan or Sitka, this summer. The four voyages r…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Fire takes 18 apartments SOUTH ANCHORAGE: 42 residents lose homes; one firefighter injured. By Lucas Wall Anchorage Daily News (Published April 28, 2001) A fire Friday afternoon tore through 18 apartments in South Anchorage, displacing 42 residents and causing hundreds of thousands of dollars in damage. Flames shoot out a window at the rear of an apartment building in the Independence Park complex. Firefighters pulled out of the building for a while because of the danger. (Marc Lester / Anchorage Daily News) No residents were injured in the blaze at 9701 Vanguard Street in the Independence Park apartment complex, though a firefighter suffered a broken nose. At least 12 people were home at 2:50 p.m. when the fire broke out. Seven of those residents were asleep at the time and awakened b…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Court fills for hearing over sisters' 747 trouble Lawyer says there's doubt over which 22-year-old caused problems By Molly Brown Anchorage Daily News (Published April 26, 2001) Attorneys applied the identical-twin defense in federal court Wednesday, arguing that sisters arrested for disrupting a United Airlines flight last week looked so much alike that no one could tell which one did what and that charges should be dismissed. Cynthia and Crystal Mikula, 22, both dressed in blue jail suits, listened to an FBI agent explain how crew members and passengers told them apart. Those on board determined Crystal Mikula was the one with the leather jacket, the agent said. And Cynthia Mikula was the one eventually restrained with plastic handcuffs. Assistant U.S. attorney Charlie Brown said tha…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Kenai prison bill starts late, moves quickly JUNEAU: $600 million plan needs more public scrutiny, critics say. By Tom Kizzia Anchorage Daily News (Published April 27, 2001) Juneau -- Two months ago, the Kenai Peninsula Borough Assembly chose its partner in a plan to develop Alaska's first private prison. Two factors set Corrections Group North ahead of its competitors, assembly members said. One was the joint venture's proposed prison site, on Native corporation land next to an existing prison. The other was the joint venture's "promotional" package, which held promise of swift legislative approval of the project. Now that promotional package, discussed in detail only behind closed doors, is bearing fruit. The state Senate is preparing to vote on a bill to allow the borough and its pr…
